svg.title: Building Refurbishment / Renovation Trade Conflict Coordination Tree
svg.desc: Static coordination tree for renovation work showing phase-based trade conflicts and mitigation priorities in existing and occupied buildings.
svg.text: BUILDING REFURBISHMENT / RENOVATION TRADE CONFLICT TREE
Existing Building Work from Selective Demo to Final Reoccupancy
Use with the Trade Conflict Coordination Framework section to compare baseline renovation interfaces against detected simultaneous-operation risks.

g.text: 1. Existing Conditions / Controls
In renovation work, schedule control, shutdown windows, tenant coordination, barriers, and temporary systems govern every downstream trade interface.
Typical pressure points: occupied egress, infection control, negative air, temporary lighting and power, hidden conditions, changing access routes.
Mitigation focus: align the sequence to shutdown and occupancy limits, maintain barrier ownership, and reverify existing conditions before work shifts.
Key interfaces: Superintendent | Infection control / logistics | Existing conditions verification | Temporary protection | Temporary utilities / HVAC
2. Selective Demolition / Hazard Removal
Selective demo, abatement, debris removal, and utility shutoffs create the first major conflicts with occupants, containment boundaries, and salvage scope.
Typical pressure points: active utilities, containment integrity, vibration-sensitive areas, elevator use, egress routes, occupied circulation.
Mitigation focus: verify utilities, control haul routes, hold adjacent trades out of containment zones, and protect salvage and finished areas.
Key interfaces: Selective demo | Structural demo / sawcutting | Abatement | Debris removal | Existing utility shutoff / lockout
3. Structural / Envelope Modifications
Shoring, structural repair, roof work, waterproofing, and window replacement must fit around limited access, occupied areas, and temporary environmental control.
Typical pressure points: weather protection, cure time, facade access, crane or hoist access, HVAC shutdowns, tenant-area protection.
Mitigation focus: confirm support and access plans, protect occupied spaces below, and sequence envelope closure with temporary environmental control.
Key interfaces: Shoring | Concrete / masonry repair | Structural reinforcement | Roofing | Waterproofing | Glazing replacement
4. Interior Rebuild / Reconfiguration
Field verification, framing, blocking, door/frame work, and ceiling-grid rework are constrained by existing geometry and hidden utilities.
Mitigation focus: remeasure after demo, confirm low-clearance routing, and verify openings and finish elevations before closure.
Key interfaces: Layout verification | Interior framing | Carpentry / blocking | Doors / hardware | Ceiling grid rework
5. MEP / System Replacement and Upgrades
MEP replacement in existing buildings adds live-system cutovers, shutdown windows, temporary service continuity, and occupied-space comfort constraints.
Typical pressure points: active panels, restroom turnover, low-clearance reroutes, sprinkler impairments, IT cutovers, device relocation, lift modernization.
Mitigation focus: stage cutovers, preserve occupied operations, define pathway ownership, and coordinate all testing and impairments by phase.
Key interfaces: Plumbing | HVAC / ductwork | Fire sprinkler | Electrical | Low voltage | Fire alarm | Security | BAS | Elevator modernization
6. Close-In / Finish Restoration
Finish restoration often overlaps with late reroutes, continuing penetrations, occupied traffic, furniture moves, and partial turnover to users.
Typical pressure points: dust-sensitive areas, moisture, finish protection, limited room access, live-system cutovers, occupied balancing and acceptance testing.
Mitigation focus: isolate occupied areas, use closure checklists, preserve access for commissioning, and protect completed finishes from late work.
Key interfaces: Firestopping / drywall | Ceilings | Flooring / tile / coatings | Millwork / specialties | Final trim | TAB / final fire alarm acceptance
7. Occupied Building / Final Turnover
Final turnover in occupied work depends on barrier control, tenant communication, room access, phased commissioning, punch completion, and life-safety approval.
Mitigation focus: keep emergency egress clear, sequence turnover by phase, and gate reoccupancy on documentation, testing, and final approvals.
Key interfaces: Infection control / dust barriers | Tenant coordination | Cleaning / punch | Testing / commissioning / training | Reoccupancy / handover
